There's a difference in your delivery lines from your flow lines as well, also calling them. That's the difference between production and execution. What I mean by that is inside of your team, whether it's in source or outsource, you're going to have people that are responsible for graphics, for video production, for producing assets and producing things like landing pages, copy, PR pieces, articles, blogs, social content, production. You see, the execution is when something's produced and given to an executioner in order to go out and put on the internet or publicize something. You see, an executioner would be somebody like a PR person in your business. They'd be somebody like the social media manager who's posting content. They would be like your digital marketer who's distributing ads after the production team is creating the assets necessary for execution. The reason that I bring up this critical difference for you in your delivery lines is because you need to understand the difference in which one and how you need to talk to these people to manage them. You see, in delivery lines, production is more of a deadline related activity, whereas execution is more of a result driven activity. You manage the statistics of these two divisions in your business differently. Production is a division that's doing something where once again they're giving a result. They're producing something, they're creating something, they're drawing something to fruition. You don't even want to call it, they're producing. Now, the production unit needs to be treated and accounted for from a perspective of how much, how many units they're producing out of there. Are they hitting deadlines on the right time? How many units are they producing? What are they producing? What's the quality of what they're producing? More importantly, after it's given to the executioner, was that efficient for the handle? You see, you need to understand these different perspectives that I just named and you need to understand how to hold these people accountable in your production unit. However, on the other side of the execution side, these people once again are held accountable from a result perspective. I'm not going to hold a digital marketer accountable by the amount of ads that he created or that she created. I'm going to hold them accountable by the result in which the ads they created produced. I'm going to hold the open rate and the clip-do rate and the conversion rate of the overall sales campaign accountable on the execution. I'm going to hold the overall creative limitations of the potential on the production unit. You see, the executioner can point fingers at the production team, but the production team can't point fingers at the execution team. You see, the production team can only point fingers at the execution team, but the team as a whole is addressed saying, why wasn't this published? The production team can say, well, the execution team didn't do it. You need to know who is responsible for what by creating a separation of these two. These two units work in tandem, of course. Sometimes it can be the same position, just doing it on both sides. But you've got to know once again how to treat both parties. All that being said, this is production versus execution in phase number six, delivery. How are you treating your people? What's your division?
